---
title: "Refine Your Plots"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

This chapter follows [Chapter 8 of @Healy2026](https://socviz.co/08-polishing.html), translating the main examples to Python, Polars, and `plotnine_polars`.

```{python}
import polars as pl
import plotnine_polars as p9
import socviz_pl as sv
import mizani.formatters as mf
from plotnine_polars import aes
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
```

The default settings that ggplot (and plotnine) ship with are generally good enough for exploratory work.
When you want to produce a figure for presentation or publication, though, you will need to make some adjustments.
Refining a plot can mean several things: adjusting the look to match your personal taste, meeting the expectations of a journal or audience, or completely transforming the appearance.
The grammar of graphics gives us a systematic way to think about these changes in terms of scales, themes, and geoms.

The `asasec` dataset records membership and revenue information for American Sociological Association (ASA) special-interest sections.

```{python}
asasec = sv.load_data("asasec")
asasec.select("Sname", "Revenues", "Members", "Journal").sample(5, seed=7)
```

We start with a basic scatterplot of section membership against revenues, with a smoothed trend line added.

```{python}
(
    asasec
    .ggplot(aes(x="Members", y="Revenues"))
    .geom_point()
    .geom_smooth()
)
```

We can map the `Journal` variable to the color aesthetic to distinguish sections that publish their own journal from those that do not.
Notice that the smooth is now fit separately for each group.

```{python}
(
    asasec
    .ggplot(aes(x="Members", y="Revenues"))
    .geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False)
    .geom_point(aes(color="Journal"))
)
```

The polished version below adds several refinements at once.
A single linear trend is drawn in a light gray so it does not compete with the points.
The axes are formatted with commas and dollar signs using formatters from `mizani`.
Sections with revenues above $7,000 are labeled by name.
Finally, the legend is moved to the bottom to leave more room for the plot area.

```{python}
(
    asasec
    .ggplot(aes(x="Members", y="Revenues"))
    .geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False, color="#CCCCCC")
    .geom_point(aes(color="Journal"))
    .geom_text(
        data=asasec.filter(pl.col("Revenues") > 7000),
        mapping=aes(label="Sname"),
        size=8,
        nudge_y=700,
    )
    .scale_x_continuous(labels=mf.comma_format())
    .scale_y_continuous(labels=mf.currency_format())
    .labs(
        x="Membership",
        y="Revenues",
        color="Section has own Journal",
        title="ASA Sections",
        subtitle="2014 Calendar year.",
        caption="Source: ASA annual report.",
    )
    .add_theme(legend_position="bottom")
)
```

## Use Colour to Your Advantage

Colour is one of the most powerful aesthetic channels in data visualization, and also one of the most misused.
The key principle is to match the palette type to the structure of the variable you are encoding.

- **Qualitative** (categorical) palettes use distinct, unordered hues for unordered categories such as countries or regime types. No color should imply "more" or "less" than another.
- **Sequential** palettes represent magnitude by moving from light to dark (or low to high saturation) along a single hue or across a narrow range of hues.
- **Diverging** palettes work for variables with a meaningful midpoint: they spread symmetrically from a neutral center toward two contrasting hues.

Matplotlib ships a large collection of named colourmaps covering all three types.
The [Matplotlib colormap reference](https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/colors/colormaps.html) is the best starting point for exploring what is available.
Plotnine gives access to these colormaps through `scale_color_cmap()` and `scale_fill_cmap()` for continuous variables, and through `scale_color_cmap_d()` and `scale_fill_cmap_d()` for discrete ones.
The classic [ColorBrewer](https://colorbrewer2.org/) palettes are available via `scale_color_brewer()` and `scale_fill_brewer()`.

### Qualitative Palettes

```{python}
organdata = sv.load_data("organdata")
```

The default discrete colour scale in plotnine (`scale_color_hue()`) spaces hues evenly around a circle in HCL space — the same approach ggplot2 uses.
For three categories it usually looks fine; with more categories the hues can become hard to distinguish.

```{python}
p = (
    organdata.drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "world"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="world"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .labs(x="Road deaths", y="Donor procurement rate", color=None)
)
p
```

ColorBrewer qualitative palettes give more deliberate control.
`Set2` uses muted tones that work well at small point sizes; `Dark2` provides higher contrast.

```{python}
p + p9.scale_color_brewer(type="qual", palette="Set2")
```

```{python}
p + p9.scale_color_brewer(type="qual", palette="Dark2")
```

### Sequential Palettes

Sequential palettes suit continuous variables.
Matplotlib's perceptually uniform colormaps — `viridis`, `plasma`, `magma`, and `cividis` — are designed to be readable in grayscale and accessible to people with the most common forms of colour blindness.
`viridis` is the default for `scale_color_cmap()`.

```{python}
(
    organdata
    .drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "gdp"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="gdp"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .scale_color_cmap("viridis")
    .labs(x="Road deaths", y="Donor procurement rate", color="GDP per capita")
)
```

ColorBrewer also has sequential palettes, accessible through `scale_color_distiller()`.

```{python}
(
    organdata.drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "gdp"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="gdp"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .scale_color_distiller(type="seq", palette="YlOrRd", direction=1)
    .labs(x="Road deaths", y="Donor procurement rate", color="GDP per capita")
)
```

### Diverging Palettes

Diverging palettes are appropriate when the data has a meaningful midpoint — zero, a historical average, the national mean — and you want to show how observations fall on either side.
Use `scale_color_gradient2()` to specify the low, mid, and high colors directly, or `scale_color_distiller()` with a diverging ColorBrewer palette.

```{python}
(
    organdata.drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "gdp"])
    .with_columns(gdp_centered=pl.col("gdp") - pl.col("gdp").mean())
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="gdp_centered"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .scale_color_gradient2(low="#2166AC", mid="white", high="#D6604D")
    .labs(
        x="Road deaths",
        y="Donor procurement rate",
        color="GDP relative\nto mean",
    )
)
```

### Manual Color Specification

Sometimes you want full control, for example to match a house style, encode a well-known political convention, or ensure a specific category is visually prominent.
`scale_color_manual()` accepts a list of color names or hex codes.
Hex values follow the format `#RRGGBB`, where each two-character pair is a base-16 number from `00` (none) to `FF` (full intensity) for the red, green, and blue channels.

```{python}
world_colors = {
    "Corporatist": "#1B9E77",
    "Liberal":     "#D95F02",
    "SocDem":      "#7570B3",
}

(
    organdata.drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "world"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="world"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .scale_color_manual(values=world_colors)
    .labs(x="Road deaths", y="Donor procurement rate", color=None)
)
```

### Color Accessibility

Many readers experience some form of color vision deficiency; the most common form makes red and green difficult to distinguish.
Matplotlib's `viridis` family of colormaps was designed with this in mind, which is one reason they are the defaults for continuous scales.
For discrete scales, the [Okabe-Ito palette](https://jfly.uni-koeln.de/color/) is a widely used eight-color qualitative palette that is distinguishable under the three most common forms of color blindness.

```{python}
okabe_ito = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
             "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#000000"]

(
    organdata.drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "world"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads", y="donors", color="world"))
    .geom_point(size=2)
    .scale_color_manual(values=okabe_ito[:3])
    .labs(x="Road deaths", y="Donor procurement rate", color=None)
)
```

## Layer, Highlight, Repeat

Aside from encoding variable values directly, color is useful for picking out or emphasizing a subset of the data.
Plotnine's layered grammar works strongly to our advantage here.
A straightforward but effective technique is to draw a base layer of all observations in a muted color, then add a second layer that highlights only the cases of interest, and finally add a text layer to label the most notable ones.

The same approach works whether you are producing a figure that stands alone or building a slide deck where you reveal layers one at a time to walk an audience through an argument.

We illustrate the technique with `organdata`.
The book's R version uses county-level 2024 U.S. election data that is not yet packaged in `socviz_pl`; we return to the same ideas about organ donation to keep the focus on the layering technique itself.

We start with a base layer plot: road deaths on the x-axis and the donor procurement rate on the y-axis.
All observations are drawn in a mid-gray with low alpha so they serve as a neutral reference backdrop.

```{python}
by_country = (
    organdata
    .drop_nulls(subset=["donors", "roads", "world", "country"])
    .group_by("country", "world")
    .agg(
        donors_mean=pl.col("donors").mean(),
        roads_mean=pl.col("roads").mean(),
    )
)

p_base = (
    by_country
    .ggplot(aes(x="roads_mean", y="donors_mean"))
    .geom_point(color="#808080", alpha=0.4, size=3)
    .labs(x="Road deaths (mean)", y="Donor procurement rate (mean)")
)
p_base
```

For the second layer we filter inside `geom_point()` to keep only countries in the Social-Democratic world (`SocDem`), mapping welfare regime to color.
These countries cluster at lower road death rates, and layering them on top of the gray backdrop makes the pattern immediately visible without hiding the full distribution.

```{python}
highlight = by_country.filter(pl.col("world") == "SocDem")

p_base + (
    p9.geom_point(data=highlight, mapping=aes(color="world"), size=3)
)
```

We add a third layer with `geom_text()`, filtering down further to the countries with the highest donor rates where a label is most useful.
The text layer is added *before* the point layer so that any leader lines it draws appear under the colored points rather than on top of them.

```{python}
labeled = by_country.filter(pl.col("donors_mean") > 20)

party_colors = {"SocDem": "#1B9E77", "Liberal": "#D95F02", "Corporatist": "#7570B3"}

(
    p_base
    + p9.geom_text(
        data=labeled,
        mapping=aes(label="country"),
        size=8,
        nudge_y=0.5,
    )
    + p9.geom_point(
        data=by_country.filter(pl.col("world").is_not_null()),
        mapping=aes(color="world"),
        size=3,
    )
    + p9.scale_color_manual(values=party_colors)
    + p9.labs(
        x="Road deaths (mean)",
        y="Donor procurement rate (mean)",
        color="Welfare regime",
        title="Organ donor procurement rates",
        caption="Countries with donor rate > 20 are labeled.",
    )
)
```

## Change the Appearance of Plots with Themes

Throughout the book we have seen how the grammar of graphics separates *what data maps to what aesthetic* from *how the non-data elements look*.
The first kind of question is answered by geoms, scales, and aesthetic mappings.
The second is the job of **themes**.

The distinction matters in practice.
Writing `geom_point(mapping=aes(size="population"))` maps point size to a data variable.
Writing `geom_point(size=3)` sets a fixed size that represents nothing in the data.
Thematic elements — background color, gridline weight, axis tick length, legend position, typeface — are always in the second category and are controlled through `theme()`.

### Built-in Themes

Plotnine ships more built-in themes than ggplot2.
The default is `theme_gray()`, but `theme_bw()`, `theme_minimal()`, `theme_classic()`, `theme_light()`, `theme_dark()`, `theme_linedraw()`, `theme_void()`, `theme_538()`, `theme_seaborn()`, `theme_matplotlib()`, and `theme_tufte()` are all available without installing any extra packages.

We build a base plot and apply several themes to it.

```{python}
import plotnine as p9

gss_lon = sv.load_data("gss_lon")

p_base = (
    asasec
    .ggplot(aes(x="Members", y="Revenues"))
    .geom_point(aes(color="Journal"), size=2)
    .labs(x="Membership", y="Revenues", color="Has journal")
)
p_base
```

```{python}
p_base + p9.theme_bw()
```

```{python}
p_base + p9.theme_minimal()
```

```{python}
p_base + p9.theme_classic()
```

`theme_tufte()` follows Edward Tufte's principle of maximizing the data-ink ratio by removing all non-data ink that does not carry information.

```{python}
p_base + p9.theme_tufte()
```

To apply a theme to every subsequent plot in a session, use `p9.theme_set()`.
This is useful at the top of a document to establish a house style without repeating the theme call on every plot.

```{python}
#| eval: false
p9.theme_set(p9.theme_minimal())
```

### Customizing Theme Elements

`theme()` takes named arguments that correspond to plot components.
Names follow a hierarchical pattern: `axis_text` affects all axis tick labels, while `axis_text_x` affects only the x-axis labels.
The value passed to each argument is one of four element functions:

- `element_text()` for text
- `element_line()` for lines and borders
- `element_rect()` for filled rectangles (backgrounds, legend boxes)
- `element_blank()` to remove an element entirely

The following example builds a faceted density plot of GSS respondent ages across survey years.
We start from the data, define a mean-age annotation table, and then progressively apply theme adjustments to strip away non-essential ink and improve readability.

```{python}
from plotnine import element_text, element_line, element_rect, element_blank

yrs = (
    list(range(1976, 1989, 2)) + [1993] +
    list(range(1996, 2019, 2)) + [2021, 2024]
)
show_years = list(range(1976, 2017, 10)) + [2024]

mean_ages = (
    gss_lon
    .drop_nulls(subset=["age"])
    .filter(pl.col("year").is_in(yrs))
    .group_by("year")
    .agg(xbar=pl.col("age").mean().round(0))
    .with_columns(y=pl.lit(0.3))
)

yr_labs = pl.DataFrame({"x": 85, "y": 0.6, "year": yrs})
```

```{python}
p_age = (
    gss_lon
    .filter(pl.col("year").is_in(yrs))
    .drop_nulls(subset=["age"])
    .ggplot(aes(x="age"))
    .geom_density(fill="#333333", color="none",
                  mapping=aes(y=p9.after_stat("scaled")))
    .geom_vline(
        data=mean_ages,
        mapping=aes(xintercept="xbar"),
        color="white",
        size=1,
    )
    .geom_text(
        data=mean_ages.filter(pl.col("year").is_in(yrs)),
        mapping=aes(x="xbar", y="y", label="xbar"),
        nudge_x=5.5,
        color="white",
        size=7,
        ha="right",
    )
    .geom_text(
        data=yr_labs.filter(pl.col("year").is_in(show_years)),
        mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", label="year"),
        size=7,
    )
    .scale_x_continuous(breaks=[20, 30, 40, 60, 70, 80])
    .facet_wrap("year", ncol=1)
    .labs(x="Age", y=None, 
          title="Age Distribution of GSS Respondents"
    )
)
p_age
```

The default output is functional but noisy for a small-multiple chart like this.
We apply `add_theme()` to remove unnecessary elements: the y-axis, facet strip labels, minor gridlines, and the panel backgrounds.

```{python}
(
    p_age
    .add_theme(
        axis_line_x=element_blank(),
        axis_text_y=element_blank(),
        axis_title_y=element_blank(),
        axis_ticks_y=element_blank(),
        strip_background=element_blank(),
        strip_text_x=element_blank(),
        strip_text_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_line(color="white", linetype="dotted"),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill="none"),
        panel_spacing_y=0,
        plot_title=element_text(size=12),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=10),
    )
)
```

### Building a Custom Theme

Rather than adding the same `theme()` call to every plot, you can wrap your adjustments in a function.
Plotnine themes compose with `+`, so the typical pattern is to start from a built-in theme and override specific elements.

```{python}
def theme_clean(base_size=11):
    return (
        p9.theme_minimal(base_size=base_size)
        + p9.theme(
            axis_line=element_line(color="#333333", size=0.3),
            panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
            panel_grid_major=element_line(color="#EEEEEE"),
            strip_background=element_rect(fill="#F5F5F5", color="#CCCCCC"),
        )
    )

p_base + theme_clean()
```

```{python}
p_base + theme_clean(base_size=14)
```

## Workhorses, Show Ponies, Unicorns

Not every plot needs the same level of polish.
A workhorse plot is one you produce quickly during exploration; a show pony is a carefully refined figure destined for a paper or presentation; a unicorn is a one-off, highly customised visualisation that required significant effort to produce.
Most of the time you want workhorses.
Occasionally one becomes a show pony.
The techniques in this section help with that transition.

### Look Both Ways

The `farsinvolved` dataset records daily counts of child pedestrians (aged 0–17) involved in fatal motor vehicle crashes in the United States from 2009 to 2023.
We are interested in the pattern across the calendar year, so we aggregate by month and day and take the mean count across years.
Because we are averaging over many years, February 29th is included; we use year 2000 as a placeholder because 2000 was a leap year.

```{python}
from datetime import date

farsinvolved = sv.load_data("farsinvolved")
studebt = sv.load_data("studebt")
okboomer = sv.load_data("okboomer")

month_order = [
    "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
    "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December",
]
month_num = {m: i + 1 for i, m in enumerate(month_order)}

fars_agg = (
    farsinvolved
    .with_columns(pl.col("day").cast(pl.Int32))
    .group_by("month", "day")
    .agg(n=pl.col("n").mean())
    .with_columns(
        month_num=pl.col("month").replace(month_num).cast(pl.Int32),
    )
    .with_columns(
        fake_yr=pl.date(2000, pl.col("month_num"), pl.col("day")),
        flag=(pl.col("month") == "October") & (pl.col("day") == 31),
    )
    .sort("fake_yr")
)
```

Halloween (October 31) stands out as the single most dangerous day for child pedestrians.
We flag it so we can color it differently in the plot.

```{python}
p9.theme_set(sv.theme_socviz())
```

```{python}
#| column: page
black_orange = {True: "#E06000", False: "#222222"}

(
    fars_agg
    .ggplot(aes(x="fake_yr", y="n", fill="flag"))
    .geom_col(width=1.05)
    .scale_fill_manual(values=black_orange)
    .add_guides(fill="none")
    .scale_x_date(
        date_breaks="1 month", 
        date_labels="%b",
        expand = (0.005, 2))
    .annotate(
        "text",
        x=date(2000, 10, 30),
        y=3.6,
        label="Halloween",
        ha="right",
        size=9,
        color=black_orange[True],
    )
    .labs(
        x="Day of the month",
        y="Mean N involved",
        title="Pedestrians aged 0–17 in Fatal Motor Vehicle Crashes",
        subtitle="Daily average, 2009–2023",
        caption="Data: NHTSA Fatality Analysis Reporting System",
    )
    .add_theme(figure_size=(10, 3))
)
```

The R version of this section also shows a polar (radial) coordinate version of the same chart using `coord_radial()` and the `geomtextpath` package.
Plotnine does not currently have a `coord_radial()` or a `geom_textsegment()` equivalent, but we can produce a similar chart using `matplotlib`'s polar projection.

```{python}
n_days = 366  # 2000 is a leap year

plot_data = (
    fars_agg
    .with_columns(doy=pl.col("fake_yr").dt.ordinal_day())
    .with_columns(angles = 2 * np.pi * (pl.col('doy') - 1) / n_days)
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 7), subplot_kw=dict(projection='polar'))
ax.set_theta_zero_location('N')
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)

ax.scatter(plot_data.filter(~pl.col('flag')).select('angles').to_series(),
           plot_data.filter(~pl.col('flag')).select('n').to_series(),
           s=15, facecolors='none', edgecolors='#222222', linewidths=0.5)
ax.scatter(plot_data.filter(pl.col('flag')).select('angles').to_series(),
           plot_data.filter(pl.col('flag')).select('n').to_series(),
           s=40, color='#E06000', zorder=5)

month_angles = [
    2 * np.pi * (date(2000, m, 1).timetuple().tm_yday - 1) / n_days
    for m in range(1, 13)
]
month_labels = [
    'January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June',
    'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December',
]
ax.set_xticks(month_angles)
ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels, size=8)
ax.set_ylim(-0.3, plot_data['n'].max() + 0.3)
ax.yaxis.set_tick_params(labelsize=7)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3, color='gray')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', pad=8)

hw_angle = 2 * np.pi * (date(2000, 10, 31).timetuple().tm_yday - 1) / n_days
hw_n = plot_data.filter(pl.col("flag")).select('n').to_series().item()
ax.annotate(
    'Halloween',
    xy=(hw_angle, hw_n),
    xytext=(hw_angle - 0.06, hw_n - 0.25),
    color='#E06000',
    fontsize=8,
    ha='center',
)
ax.set_title(
    "Pedestrians aged 0–17 in Fatal Motor Vehicle Crashes\nDaily average, 2009–2023",
    pad=15,
    size=9,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The column chart above conveys the same information more directly: lengths are easier to judge than angles.

### Saying No to Pie

Pie charts ask readers to judge angles and arc lengths, which people do poorly compared to judging position along a common scale or the length of a bar.
The `studebt` dataset describes the distribution of outstanding U.S. student loan debt, broken into debt-amount bands, with separate rows for the share of borrowers and the share of total balances in each band.

```{python}
studebt.head(8)
```

A faceted bar chart is a straightforward alternative to a pie chart.
Each panel shows one distribution, and within each panel the bar lengths are directly comparable.

```{python}
debt_order = [
    "Under $5k", "$5k-$10k", "$10k-$25k", "$25k-$50k",
    "$50k-$75k", "$75k-$100k", "$100k-$200k", "Over $200k",
]
f_labs = {
    "Borrowers": "Percent of all Borrowers",
    "Balances":  "Percent of all Balances",
}

p_caption = "Source: FRB NY"
p_title   = "Outstanding Student Loans"
p_subtitle = "44 million borrowers owe a total of $1.3 trillion"

(
    studebt
    .with_columns(
        pct_prop=pl.col("pct") / 100,
        Debtrc=pl.col("Debtrc").cast(pl.Enum(debt_order)),
    )
    .ggplot(aes(x="pct_prop", y="Debtrc", fill="type"))
    .geom_col()
    .scale_x_continuous(labels=mf.percent_format())
    .scale_fill_brewer(type="qual", palette="Set2")
    .add_guides(fill="none")
    .facet_grid(". ~ type", labeller=dict(zip(f_labs.keys(), f_labs.values())))
    .labs(
        x=None, y=None,
        caption=p_caption, title=p_title, subtitle=p_subtitle,
    )
    .add_theme(strip_text_x=element_text(face="bold"))
)
```

A stacked horizontal bar is another option.
It sacrifices within-bar comparisons (each segment is harder to read than in the faceted version) but makes it easier to see that the two distributions sum to 100% and to compare their shapes at a glance.

```{python}
(
    studebt
    .with_columns(
        pct_prop=pl.col("pct") / 100,
        Debtrc=pl.col("Debtrc").cast(pl.Enum(debt_order)),
    )
    .ggplot(aes(x="pct_prop", y="type", fill="Debtrc"))
    .geom_col(color="white", position="stack")
    .scale_x_continuous(labels=mf.percent_format())
    .scale_fill_brewer(type="qual", palette="Set3", direction=-1)
    .labs(
        x=None, y=None,
        fill="Dollar amount owed",
        caption=p_caption, title=p_title, subtitle=p_subtitle,
    )
    .add_theme(legend_position="top")
)
```

### Build More Complex Layouts

The `patchwork` R package lets you arrange separate ggplot objects into a single figure with a shared layout.
Python does not have a direct equivalent built into plotnine, but the same result can usually be achieved by reshaping data into a long format and using `facet_grid()` with `scales="free_y"`.
The key constraint is that every panel shares the same geom; when you need genuinely different geoms per panel the matplotlib `gridspec` API or the `patchworklib` package are options worth exploring.

The `okboomer` dataset contains a time-series decomposition of monthly U.S. birth rates from 1933 to 2015.
We filter to the pre-1976 baby-boom period, pivot the three decomposition components into a long format, and facet by component.

```{python}
component_order = ["trend", "seasonal", "remainder"]
component_labels = {
    "trend":     "Trend",
    "seasonal":  "Seasonal",
    "remainder": "Remainder",
}

okboomer_long = (
    okboomer
    .filter(pl.col("date") < pl.date(1976, 1, 1))
    .unpivot(
        index=["date"],
        on=component_order,
        variable_name="component",
        value_name="value",
    )
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("component").cast(pl.Enum(component_order)),
    )
)

(
    okboomer_long
    .ggplot(aes(x="date", y="value"))
    .geom_line(size=0.5)
    .facet_grid(
        "component ~ .",
        # scales="free_y",
        labeller=dict(zip(component_order,
                          [component_labels[c] for c in component_order])),
    )
    .scale_x_date(
        date_breaks="10 years",
        date_labels="%Y",
    )
    .labs(
        x="Year", y=None,
        title="U.S. Birth Rates, 1933–1975",
        caption="Trend, seasonal, and remainder components of monthly births per million.",
    )
    .add_theme(
        strip_text_y=element_text(angle=0, ha="left"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=7),
    )
)
```
